results = []

So this type of look is called "list comprehension"  It's a really cool and powerful part of python: http://treyhunner.com/2015/12/python-list-comprehensions-now-in-color/

In this case, the loop is saying: Create a new list of items that are just the ``revision`` field from each item in the ``results`` list.  And the ``set`` part around it takes that new list and make it into a ``set`` object instead.  A ``list`` can have duplicates, but a ``set`` can't.  So making it into a ``set`` will automatically remove all dupes.  The cost is that it will lose the order because ``sets`` don't have order.  But in this case, we don't care about order.

I like math puzzles like this, so I was playing with it last night.  This is what I came up with:

```
    """
    HTTP ranges use an *inclusive* index at the end.
    """

    def __init__(self, start, end):
        assert 0 <= start <= end
        self.start = start
        self.end = end

    def size(self):
        return self.end - self.start + 1

    def subrange(self, sub_start, sub_end):
        """
        Returns a range that is part of this range.
        :param sub_start: Index relative to the start of this range.
        :param sub_end: (Inclusive!) index relative to the start of this range.
        :return: A new Range
        """
        assert 0 <= sub_start <= sub_end < self.size()
        return Range(self.start + sub_start, self.start + sub_end)

    def __repr__(self):
        return 'Range(%d, %d)' % (self.start, self.end)


class PartToDownload(object):
    """
    Holds the range of a file to download, and the range of the
    local file where it should be stored.
    """
    def __init__(self, cloud_range, local_range):
        self.cloud_range = cloud_range
        self.local_range = local_range

    def __repr__(self):
        return 'PartToDownload(%s, %s)' % (self.cloud_range, self.local_range)


def gen_parts(cloud_range, local_range, part_count):
    """
    Generates a sequence of PartToDownload to download a large file as
    a collection of parts.
    """
    assert cloud_range.size() == local_range.size()
    assert 0 < part_count <= cloud_range.size()
    offset = 0
    remaining_size = cloud_range.size()
    for i in range(part_count):
        # This rounds down, so if the parts aren't all the same size,
        # the smaller parts will come first.
        this_part_size = remaining_size // (part_count - i)
        yield PartToDownload(
            cloud_range.subrange(offset, offset + this_part_size - 1),
            local_range.subrange(offset, offset + this_part_size - 1)
        )
        offset += this_part_size
        remaining_size -= this_part_size
```

It's more than just "unset a flag before any handlers run".

It's also a way for layers to use flags without having to worry about what upper or lower layers might do with those flags. With a trigger, a layer can say "**I want to run when the config has changed even if another handler already removed that flag.**" The layer creates the following trigger.

```python
trigger(
    event='config.changed'
    set='mylayer.config.changed')
```

That layer is then free to remove `mylayer.config.changed` whenever it wants without having to worry of suppressing `config.changed` for other layers.

The weird behavior of the `config.changed` flag in reactive 1.0 is solved by triggers. That's why I'm so exited about triggers. It fixes all the problems and inconsistencies we have in reactive 1.0. We don't need an extra `file_changed` or a `resource_changed` decorator. These can all be implemented using triggers with custom events. No need to put additional decorators in the reactive framework. Layer devs can fix their own problems. Less work for us and more freedom for layer devs to experiment. Who knows what kind of events they might find useful?

Another example is the behavior @stub42 wants for the apt layer; he wants to know if landscape removed a packages. The apt packages changing becomes a custom event that triggers a flag.

I haven't elaborated on custom events, maybe I should also write a draft for that.

This has to be part of Marshmallow itself as it sets up the contract for dealing with the external library, otherwise the external library has to duplicate huge swaths of this file to inject itself.  Doing this via hooks and a contract creates a much looser coupling with Marshmallow and the external library.

The other main issue with the subclass approach is that it doesn't allow some of the other great libraries in the Marshmallow ecosystem to benefit from this.  For example there's no way for me to compose a `flask_marshmallow.Schema` with a `toastedmarshmallow.JitSchema` without effectively doubling the class hierarchy in all of those other frameworks.

> The value of some objects can change. Objects whose value can change are said to be mutable; objects whose value is unchangeable once they are created are called immutable. (The value of an immutable container object that contains a reference to a mutable object can change when the latter’s value is changed; however the container is still considered immutable, because the collection of objects it contains cannot be changed.
